q: The mass of proton, neutron and helium nucleus are respectively <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0073</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0087</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mo> </mo><mi>and</mi><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0015</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></math>. The binding energy of helium nucleus is:
question: The mass of proton, neutron and helium nucleus are respectively <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0073</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0087</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mo> </mo><mi>and</mi><mo> </mo><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0015</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></math>. The binding energy of helium nucleus is:
solution:
Given here,  mHe=4.0015 u, mp=1.0073 u and mn=1.0087 u.

Mass defect

 ∆m = 2mp+2mn-mHe=2×1.0073+2×1.0087-4.0015=0.0305 u

Binding energy 

E = ∆m×931 MeV=0.0305×931=28.4 MeV

The ionization potential of this hydrogen atom will be:-
question: Imagine that the electron in a hydrogen atom is replaced by a muon <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>μ</mi></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math> The mass of muon particle is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>207</mn></math> times that of an electron and charge is equal to the charge of an electron. The ionization potential of this hydrogen atom will be:-
solution:
E∝1r⇒r∝1 m⇒E∝m. Ionization potential =13.6×MassμeVMasse
=13.6×207eV=2815.2eV.

subject: Physics
q: The energy of an electron in an orbit of the Bohr's atom is $-0.04 E_{0} \mathrm{eV}$ where $E_{0}$ is the ground state energy. If $L$ is the angular momentum of the electron in this orbit and $h$ is the Planck's constant, then $\frac{2 \pi L}{h}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ :
question: The energy of an electron in an orbit of the Bohr's atom is $-0.04 E_{0} \mathrm{eV}$ where $E_{0}$ is the ground state energy. If $L$ is the angular momentum of the electron in this orbit and $h$ is the Planck's constant, then $\frac{2 \pi L}{h}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ :
solution:
In Bohr's atom, the energy levels are $E_n = -\frac{E_0}{n^2}$ where $E_0$ is the ground state energy.
Given $E = -0.04E_0$, we have $-\frac{E_0}{n^2} = -0.04E_0$, which gives $n^2 = 25$, so $n = 5$.
The angular momentum in Bohr's model is $L = n\hbar = n\frac{h}{2\pi}$.
Therefore, $\frac{2\pi L}{h} = \frac{2\pi}{h} \times n\frac{h}{2\pi} = n = 5$.

q: Considering the Bohr model of hydrogen like atoms, the ratio of the ratio of the radius $5^{\text {th }}$ orbit of the electron in $\mathrm{Li}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{He}^{+}$is
question: Considering the Bohr model of hydrogen like atoms, the ratio of the ratio of the radius $5^{\text {th }}$ orbit of the electron in $\mathrm{Li}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{He}^{+}$is
solution:
$\mathrm{r}=\mathrm{r} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{n}^2}{2}$
for $\mathrm{Li}^{2+}$
$r_5=r \cdot \frac{25}{3}$
for $\mathrm{He}^{+}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{r}_5=\mathrm{r} \cdot \frac{25}{2} \\ & \therefore \frac{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{Li}^{2+}}}{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{He}^{+}}}=\frac{2}{3}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The shortest wavelength of the spectral lines in the Lyman series of hydrogen spectrum is $915 Ã…$. The longest wavelength of spectral lines in the Balmer series will be _____ $Ã…$.
question: The shortest wavelength of the spectral lines in the Lyman series of hydrogen spectrum is $915 Ã…$. The longest wavelength of spectral lines in the Balmer series will be _____ $Ã…$.
solution:
Shortest, $\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}=-13.6\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_1^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_2^2}\right)$ $\lambda \downarrow \mathrm{E} \uparrow ; \frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_0}=-13.6(1)$ Balmer Series :
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_1}=-13.6\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right) \\ & \frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_1}=-13.6\left(\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{9}\right) \end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_1}=-13.6 \times\left(\frac{5}{36}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{-13.6 \lambda_0}{\lambda_1}=-13.6 \times \frac{5}{36} \\ & \lambda_1=\frac{\lambda_0 \times 36}{5}=\frac{915 \times 36}{5}=6588\end{aligned}$

q: The energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>He</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> ion in its first state is, (The ground state energy for the Hydrogen atom<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>13</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi></math>):
question: The energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>He</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> ion in its first state is, (The ground state energy for the Hydrogen atom<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>13</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi></math>):
solution:
Energy of Hydrogen like atoms is, E=-13.6Z2n2eV.

Since Helium is in the first state, Z=2, n=2.

The energy of He+ ion is,

E=-13.6×2222=-13.6 eV

subject: Physics
q: The energy required to remove the electron from a singly ionized Helium atom is $2.2$ times the energy required to remove an electron from Helium atom. The total energy required to ionize the Helium atom completely is:
question: The energy required to remove the electron from a singly ionized Helium atom is $2.2$ times the energy required to remove an electron from Helium atom. The total energy required to ionize the Helium atom completely is:
solution:
Energy required to remove $\mathrm{e}^{-}$from singly ionized helium atom $=\frac{(13.6) Z^2}{1^2}=54.4 \mathrm{eV}$ $(\because \mathrm{Z}=2)$ Energy required to remove $\mathrm{e}^{-}$from helium atom $=x \mathrm{eV}$
According to question, $54.4 \mathrm{eV}=2.2 \mathrm{x} \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}$ $=24.73 \mathrm{eV}$
Therefore, energy required to ionize helium atom $=(54.4+24.73) \mathrm{eV}=79.12 \mathrm{eV}$

subject: Physics
q: A particle of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> moves in a circular orbit in a central potential field <math><mi>U</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mi>k</mi><msup><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> If Bohr's quantization conditions are applied, radii of possible orbitals and energy levels vary with quantum number <math><mi>n</mi></math> as:
question: A particle of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> moves in a circular orbit in a central potential field <math><mi>U</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mi>k</mi><msup><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> If Bohr's quantization conditions are applied, radii of possible orbitals and energy levels vary with quantum number <math><mi>n</mi></math> as:
solution:
Fr=-dUdr=-kr

For circular motion

|Fr|=kr=mv2r⇒kr2=mv2 ........(i)

Bohr's quantization ⇒mvr=nh2π ....(ii)

From (i) and (ii)

m2v2m=kr2

⇒1mnh2πr2=kr2⇒n2h24π2mk=r4

⇒r=h24π2mk1/4 n1/2

r∝n

From equation (i) U∝n

KE=12mv2; PE=12kr2

E=K+U=12mv2+12kr2=kr2∝n

hcλα=13.614-19   ...1

The formula to calculate the wavelength of Hβ-line in Balmer series is given by

=3664×125

B= μI 2R

B= μ 0 2 e 2π v R 2

B= μ 0 e 4π z n ( n 2 z ) 2

B=μ0e4πz3n5

B∝ n −5 .

question: Both the nucleus and the atom of some element are in their respective first excited states. The ratio <math><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>N</mi></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac></math> is closest to:
solution: <math><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>N</mi></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>a</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mi> </mi><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>a</mi></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>N</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac></math>, where <math><msub><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>a</mi></mrow></msub><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi> </mi><msub><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>N</mi></mrow></msub></math> are energies of photons from atom and nucleus respectively. <math><msub><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>N</mi></mrow></msub></math> is of the order of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>MeV</mi></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>a</mi></mrow></msub></math> in few <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>eV</mi></math>. So, <math><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>N</mi></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>λ</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>a</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup></math>.

subject: Physics
q: Two electrons are moving with non-relativistic speeds perpendicular to each other. If corresponding de Broglie wavelengths are $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$, their de Broglie wavelength in the frame of reference attached to their centre of mass is:
question: Two electrons are moving with non-relativistic speeds perpendicular to each other. If corresponding de Broglie wavelengths are $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$, their de Broglie wavelength in the frame of reference attached to their centre of mass is:
solution:
Momentum (p) of each electron $\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\lambda_1} \hat{\mathrm{i}}$ and $\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\lambda_2} \hat{\mathrm{j}}$
Velocity of centre of mass
$$
\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{cm}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{2 \mathrm{~m} \lambda_1} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\frac{\mathrm{h}}{2 \mathrm{~m} \lambda_2} \hat{\mathrm{j}} \quad(\because \mathrm{p}=\mathrm{mv})
$$
Velocity of 1 st particle about centre of mass
$$
V_{1 \mathrm{~cm}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{2 \mathrm{~m} \lambda_1} \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\frac{\mathrm{h}}{2 \mathrm{~m} \lambda_2} \hat{\mathrm{j}}
$$
$\lambda_{\mathrm{cm}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{h}^2}{4 \lambda_1^2}+\frac{\mathrm{h}^2}{4 \lambda_2^2}}}=\frac{2 \lambda_1 \lambda_2}{\sqrt{\lambda_1^2+\lambda_2^2}} \quad\left(\because \lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{p}}\right)$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: Most of the mass of the atom and all its positive charge are concentrated in a tiny nucleus and the electrons revolve around it, is Rutherford's model.
Statement II: An atom is a spherical cloud of positive charges with electrons embedded in it, is a special case of Rutherford's model.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: Most of the mass of the atom and all its positive charge are concentrated in a tiny nucleus and the electrons revolve around it, is Rutherford's model.
Statement II: An atom is a spherical cloud of positive charges with electrons embedded in it, is a special case of Rutherford's model.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate from the options given below.
options: Both statement I and statement II are false
Statement I is false but statement II is true
Statement I is true but statement II is false
Both statement I and statement II are true
solution:
Rutherford proposed that most of the mass of an atom is concentrated in a small, dense nucleus at the centre, which contains positively charged protons. Electrons, carrying negative charge, revolve around the nucleus in orbits.

In the Bohr model, electrons do not move in random orbits but have specific energy levels or shells around the nucleus. The electrons are not in a "spherical cloud" but are found in discrete orbits at fixed distances from the nucleus. Rutherford's model, on the other hand, did not specify the fixed orbits of electrons and was later refined by Bohr to explain the stability of atoms.

Hence, statement I is true but statement II is false.

subject: Physics
q: In a hydrogen like ion, the energy difference between the $2^{\text {nd }}$ excitation energy state and ground is 108.8 eV. The atomic number of the ion is
question: In a hydrogen like ion, the energy difference between the $2^{\text {nd }}$ excitation energy state and ground is 108.8 eV. The atomic number of the ion is
solution:
$\Delta \mathrm{E}=13.6 \mathrm{z}^2\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_1^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_2^2}\right]$
$(13.6) \mathrm{z}^2\left[\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{9}\right]=108.8$
$\frac{(13.6)(8)}{9}\left(z^2\right)=108.8$
$\mathrm{z}=3$

subject: Physics
q: A hydrogen atom, initially in the ground state is excited by absorbing a photon of wavelength $980 Ã…$. The radius of the atom in the excited state, in terms of Bohr radius $a_{0}$, will be:
question: A hydrogen atom, initially in the ground state is excited by absorbing a photon of wavelength $980 Ã…$. The radius of the atom in the excited state, in terms of Bohr radius $a_{0}$, will be:
solution:
Energy of photon $=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}=\frac{12500}{980}=12.75 \mathrm{eV}$

Energy of electron in $\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$ orbit is given by

$\mathrm{En}=\frac{-13.6}{\mathrm{n}^{2}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{n}}-\mathrm{E}_{1}=-13.6\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}^{2}} \frac{-1}{1^{2}}\right]$

$\Rightarrow 12.75=13.6\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}} \frac{-1}{\mathrm{n}^{2}}\right] \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=4$

$\therefore$ Electron will excite to $\mathrm{n}=4$ We know that ' $R^{\prime} \propto n^{2}$

$\therefore$ Radius of atom will be $16 \mathrm{a}_{0}$

q: For a hydrogen atom, the ratio of the largest wavelength of Lyman series to that of the Balmer series is.
question: For a hydrogen atom, the ratio of the largest wavelength of Lyman series to that of the Balmer series is.
solution:
$\mathrm {Lyman}$

$\frac{1}{\lambda_1}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{4}\right]=\frac{3 \mathrm{R}}{4}$
$\lambda_1=\frac{4}{3 R}$ ________...(1)
and $\mathrm {Balmer}$
$\frac{1}{\lambda_2}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{9}\right]=\frac{5 \mathrm{R}}{36}$
$\lambda_2=\frac{36}{5 R}$
Then, $\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{5}{27}$

subject: Physics
q: The longest wavelength associated with Paschen series is : (Given $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}}=1.097 \times 10^7 \mathrm{SI}$ unit)
question: The longest wavelength associated with Paschen series is : (Given $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}}=1.097 \times 10^7 \mathrm{SI}$ unit)
solution:
For longest wavelength in Paschen's series:
$\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_1^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_2^2}\right]$
For longest $\mathrm{n}_1=3$
$\mathrm{n}_2=4$
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{(3)^2}-\frac{1}{(4)^2}\right] \\ & \frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{9}-\frac{1}{16}\right] \\ & \frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{16-9}{16 \times 9}\right] \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{16 \times 9}{7 \mathrm{R}}=\frac{16 \times 9}{7 \times 1.097 \times 10^7} \\ & \lambda=1.876 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A diatomic molecule is made of two masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ which are separated by a distance $r$. If we calculate its rotational energy by applying Bohr's rule of angular momentum quantization, its energy will be given by ( $n$ is an integer)
question: A diatomic molecule is made of two masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ which are separated by a distance $r$. If we calculate its rotational energy by applying Bohr's rule of angular momentum quantization, its energy will be given by ( $n$ is an integer)
solution:
$r_1=\frac{m_2 r}{m_1+m_2} ; r_2=\frac{m_1 r}{m_1+m_2}$
$\left(l_1+l_2\right) \omega=\frac{n h}{2 \pi}=nh$
$K . E=\frac{1}{2}\left(l_1+l_2\right) \omega^2=\frac{n^2 h^2\left(m_1+m_2\right)}{2 m_1 m_2 r^2}$

q:
xx+4 is the ratio of energies of photons produced due to transition of an electron of hydrogen atom from its

(i) third permitted energy level to the second level and

(ii) the highest permitted energy level to the second permitted level.

The value of x will be
question:
xx+4 is the ratio of energies of photons produced due to transition of an electron of hydrogen atom from its

(i) third permitted energy level to the second level and

(ii) the highest permitted energy level to the second permitted level.

The value of x will be
solution:
Energy of a photon released in transition of electron in hydrogen atom is given by,

E=13.61n22-1n12 eV

⇒E1E2=13.614-1913.614-1∞=xx+4⇒14-1914=xx+4

⇒59=xx+4

⇒5x+20=9x

⇒x=5

subject: Physics
q:
A beam of monochromatic light is used to excite the electron in Li++ from the first orbit to the third orbit. The wavelength of monochromatic light is found to be x×10-10 m. The value of x is _____ .

[Given hc=1242 eV nm]
question:
A beam of monochromatic light is used to excite the electron in Li++ from the first orbit to the third orbit. The wavelength of monochromatic light is found to be x×10-10 m. The value of x is _____ .

[Given hc=1242 eV nm]
solution:
Energy released in the transition will be,

ΔE=13.6×Z2112-132  =13.6×32×89 eV=108.8 eV

Now energy of photon can be written as,

hcλ=108.8 eV ⇒λ=12400108.8 Ao≈114 Ao

subject: Physics
q: Hydrogen atom is bombarded with electrons accelerated through a potential different of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math>, which causes excitation of hydrogen atoms. If the experiment is being formed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>. The minimum potential difference needed to observe any Balmer series lines in the emission spectra will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>α</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo></math>_________.(Write the value to the nearest integer)
question: Hydrogen atom is bombarded with electrons accelerated through a potential different of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math>, which causes excitation of hydrogen atoms. If the experiment is being formed at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math>. The minimum potential difference needed to observe any Balmer series lines in the emission spectra will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>α</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo></math>_________.(Write the value to the nearest integer)
solution:
For minimum potential difference, electron has to make transition from n=3 to n=2  state but first electron has to reach to n=3 state from ground state. So, energy of bombarding electron should be equal to energy difference of n=3 and n=1 state.

ΔE=13.61-132e=eV

⇒13.6×89=V

⇒V=12.09 V≈12.1 V

So, α=121.

q: The first three spectral lines of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math>-atom in the Balmer series are given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> considering the Bohr atomic model, the wave lengths of first and third spectral lines <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfrac></mfenced></math> are related by a factor of approximately <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>, to the nearest integer, is ________.
question: The first three spectral lines of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math>-atom in the Balmer series are given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> considering the Bohr atomic model, the wave lengths of first and third spectral lines <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>λ</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfrac></mfenced></math> are related by a factor of approximately <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>, to the nearest integer, is ________.
solution:
1λ1=Rz2122-132

1λ1=Rz2536   ...(i)

For 3rd line

1λ3=Rz2122-152

1λ3=Rz221100   ...(ii)

(ii)+(i)

λ1λ3=21100×365=1.512=15.12×10-1

x≈15
